transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article objectively and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words, neutral tone)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items — the 5 most objectively significant facts
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective or source type included
- Do not editorialize, adopt the article's framing, or use loaded language
- Represent all perspectives present in the article proportionally

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S
SOURCE: Newsweek

ARTICLE:

By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S. President Donald Trump has said the U.S. is "seeking immediate negotiations" to acquire Greenland, deepening the yawning chasm opening up between Washington and its closest allies over the Arctic territory.Trump called Greenland "part of North America" and "our territory" during a rambling speech to world leaders at the World Economic Forum in the Swiss town of Davos on Wednesday.The Republican reiterated his claim that the U.S. is the only power that can protect the mostly ice-covered expanse, and insisted this would not pose a threat to NATO.Alliance officials have repeatedly said a U.S. military attack on Greenland, a semi-autonomous part of Denmark, would spell the end of NATO. The prospect of the U.S. — the most influential and powerful member of NATO —attacking territory belonging to a fellow alliance member has always been unfathomable, until now.The White House had refused to rule out using military force to take Greenland, which would be a violation of international law."I don't want to use force. I won't use force," Trump said. "All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland."This is a breaking story. More to follow....Request Reprint & LicensingSubmit CorrectionView Editorial & AI GuidelinesAdd Newsweek as a preferred source on Google to see more of our trusted coverage when you search.Related PodcastsTop StoriesFor MembersWorldWhy the US Won't Be Coming to Its Longtime Ally's Aid in Syria10 min readFor MembersNewsUncommon Knowledge: China Complaining About Trump's Land Grab? That's Rich5 min readNewsAnti-ICE Resistance Raises Prospect of Government Shutdown6 min readFor MembersOpinionDonald Trump Finally Makes the Right Move on Greenland: 'Immediate Negotiations'5 min readNewsPam Bondi Announces Departure of Lindsey Halligan From DOJ3 min readPoliticsMaryland's New Congressional Map Could Wipe Republicans Out4 min readTrendingDon BaconRepublican Blasts 'Immoral' Trump Actions After Issuing Impeachment Warning7 min readViralShopper Finds $4.99 Sweater in Thrift Store—Then Realizes Royal Connection3 min readDonald TrumpDonald Trump Leaks Private Texts From NATO Allies: Read in Full6 min read25th AmendmentDemocrats Call to Invoke 25th Amendment Against Donald Trump6 min readNcaaCarson Beck Faces Backlash for Postgame Decision After Miami's Loss to Indiana2 min readOpinionOpinionShould the US Put Boots on the Ground in Iran? Newsweek Contributors Debate6 min readOpinionSyria's Kurds Lose Out | Opinion6 min readOpinionAgriculture Secretary: A Year of Results—How the Trump Admin Put American Farmers First | Opinion6 min readOpinionThe New American ICE Age | Opinion5 min readFor MembersOpinionConventional Wisdom: Greenland Edition3 min read
---

{
  "summary": "U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the United States is actively seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, a territory currently under Danish sovereignty. Speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Trump referred to Greenland as part of North America and implied it to be connected to U.S. interests, while dismissing the notion that U.S. intentions would threaten NATO. The White House had previously declined to rule out the use of military force to claim the territory, an action that NATO officials indicated would signal the dissolution of the alliance, as it would constitute an attack on a member’s land and violate international law. Trump, however, clarified in his remarks that he does not intend to use force, stating that all the United States seeks is the ownership of Greenland. The story unfolds as tensions rise between the U.S., Denmark, and other NATO members regarding the future of Greenland and broader Arctic geopolitics, with further developments expected.",
  "key_facts": [
    "President Trump stated the U.S. is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland.",
    "Trump characterized Greenland as part of North America and linked it to U.S. interests.",
    "He insisted U.S. intentions toward Greenland would not threaten NATO.",
    "NATO officials warned a U.S. military move on Greenland, a Danish territory, could spell the end of NATO.",
    "Trump clarified he does not intend to use military force to obtain Greenland."
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "U.S. government statements",
    "NATO officials",
    "Newsweek reporting"
  ]
}

parsed_output.summary: U.S. President Donald Trump announced that the United States is actively seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, a territory currently under Danish sovereignty. Speaking at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Trump referred to Greenland as part of North America and implied it to be connected to U.S. interests, while dismissing the notion that U.S. intentions would threaten NATO. The White House had previously declined to rule out the use of military force to claim the territory, an action that NATO officials indicated would signal the dissolution of the alliance, as it would constitute an attack on a member’s land and violate international law. Trump, however, clarified in his remarks that he does not intend to use force, stating that all the United States seeks is the ownership of Greenland. The story unfolds as tensions rise between the U.S., Denmark, and other NATO members regarding the future of Greenland and broader Arctic geopolitics, with further developments expected.
key_facts: President Trump stated the U.S. is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland.
Trump characterized Greenland as part of North America and linked it to U.S. interests.
He insisted U.S. intentions toward Greenland would not threaten NATO.
NATO officials warned a U.S. military move on Greenland, a Danish territory, could spell the end of NATO.
Trump clarified he does not intend to use military force to obtain Greenland.
